metadata.header_path: /Medial Knee Pain: Medial Collateral Ligament / Medial Meniscus/
/KINESIOLOGY TAPING TECHNIQUES FOR THE KNEE JOINT/
/General Knee Pain / Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome/

__data__.text:
## General Knee Pain / Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome

Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS) is a painful condition that can relate to a type of mal-tracking of the kneecap (patella) (see Fig. 3.1). There are many causes for this condition, such as an over-pronation of the subtalar joint (STJ) of the ankle and poor foot biomechanics. Weakness of the inner quadriceps muscle (called the vastus medialis) can also contribute to PFPS, especially the vastus medialis oblique (VMO) fibers, which are thought to atrophy due to pain and minimal swelling. In addition, weak gluteus medius (Gmed) and gluteus maximus (Gmax) can cause this type of knee pain. The knee joint is therefore what I refer to as “a weak link in the kinetic chain,” and typically the presentation of the pain is not where the problem lies.

> **Figure 3.1**: General knee pain/PFPS.

metadata.header_path: /General Knee Pain / Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome/

__data__.text:
### Taping Procedure: Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome

| Step | Application Path | Position | Stretch % |
|------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Attach a “Y” strip from the superior aspect of the patella and apply the tape to the medial and lateral sides of the patella, finishing by crossing over the tibial tuberosity | Long sitting, knee at 90° flexion | 0% |
| 2 | Apply a “Y” strip from the tibial tuberosity, lay the tape medially and laterally around the patella to overlap the first “Y” application, finishing near the quadriceps tendon | Long sitting, knee at 90° flexion | Little to no stretch |
| 3 | Heat activate the glue | — | — |
| 4 | Lower the limb back down to the couch and observe the “wrinkling” of the tape | — | — |

> **Figure 3.2**: First application of the “Y” strip starting from the superior aspect of the patella and finishing at the tibial tuberosity.

> **Figure 3.3**: Second application of the “Y” strip starting from the tibial tuberosity and finishing at the starting point of the first application.

Knee pain (patellofemoral pain syndrome)

Chen et al. (2008) conducted a study of the “Biomechanics effects of Kinesio Taping for persons with patellofemoral pain syndrome during stair climbing.” They concluded that this type of kinesiology taping could reduce pain and improve the ratio of VMO/vastus lateralis (VL) for the mechanism of patellar stability during stair climbing.

In general, PFPS is possibly one of the most common conditions that I have treated with some form of strapping and taping. This includes conventional athletic taping methods using zinc oxide (Z/O), as with the McConnell technique to control patellar alignment, as well as kinesiology taping. I like and use all the varieties of taping techniques as each of them has a unique effect upon the knee. However, my choice at the present time for helping to reduce athletes’ and patients’ persistent knee pains is the kinesiology taping method.

---

metadata.header_path: /General Knee Pain / Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome/
/Full Knee Taping: PFPS / Patellar Tendinopathy / Osgood-Schlatter’s Disease / Bursitis/

__data__.text:
## Full Knee Taping: PFPS / Patellar Tendinopathy / Osgood-Schlatter’s Disease / Bursitis

Any athlete who presents with pain inferior to the patella may also have pain in other soft tissues, such as the soft-tissue structure known as the patellar tendon. However, the pain could be due to other associated structures that sit on top of the tibial plateau, e.g., fat pad, infrapatellar bursa, and even the meniscus. 

If, for example, a basketball player presents with pain to this area then it can be assumed that they have a condition called “jumper’s knee,” which is simply a tendinopathy of the patellar tendon. If, however, a 14-year-old footballer comes to the clinic with pain on the insertion of the patellar tendon at the attachment to the tibial tuberosity, then this is more than likely Osgood-Schlatter’s disease (Fig. 3.4).

This following technique is known as “full knee taping” as it has the ability to give a bit more stability without restricting the movement. It is an exceptional technique that can be quickly incorporated into your treatment program to offload the pain localized at the inferior aspect of the patella, as well as controlling the position of the patellofemoral joint.

> **Figure 3.4**: General knee pain/PFPS. Patellar tendinopathy/bursitis. Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS). Osgood-Schlatter’s disease.

metadata.header_path: /Full Knee Taping: PFPS / Patellar Tendinopathy / Osgood-Schlatter’s Disease / Bursitis/

__data__.text:
### Taping Procedure: Full Knee Taping

| Step | Application Path | Position | Stretch % |
|------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Apply a small “I” strip across the patellar tendon | Long sitting, knee at 90° flexion | 50–75% |
| 2 | Apply an “I” strip from the lateral aspect of the thigh toward the patella, then 50% stretch from lateral side of patella to anterior knee, finish by crossing tibial tuberosity with no stretch at the end | Long sitting, knee at 90° flexion | 25% (thigh to patella), 50% (lateral patella to anterior knee), 0% (end) |
| 3 | Apply an “I” strip from the medial aspect of the thigh toward the patella, then 50% stretch from medial side of patella to front of knee, crossing tibial tuberosity with no stretch at the end | Long sitting, knee at 90° flexion | 25% (thigh to patella), 50% (medial patella to front of knee), 0% (end) |
| 4 | Heat activate the glue | — | — |

> **Figure 3.5**: Small “I” strip applied across the patellar tendon.

> **Figure 3.6**: An “I” strip is applied from the lateral aspect of the thigh to the tibial tuberosity.

> **Figure 3.7**: An “I” strip is applied from the medial aspect of the thigh to the tibial tuberosity.

Knee pain (patellofemoral syndrome/Osgood-Schlatter’s disease)

---

metadata.header_path: /Full Knee Taping: PFPS / Patellar Tendinopathy / Osgood-Schlatter’s Disease / Bursitis/
/Knee Malalignment Taping Technique/

__data__.text:
## Knee Malalignment Taping Technique

The two techniques demonstrated below are potentially very simple in one respect because only one piece of tape is required. I do find in practice that sometimes the simple approach can work very well. Just remember that the majority of knee pain is merely a symptom, and the causative factor for the patient’s pain might be in another structure, such as the hip joint, or possibly over-pronation of the foot.

The application of a single piece of tape can be done on its own with a 2-inch small “I” strip. The technique can also be applied in combination with the full knee taping, as described below. Another option is to use the 4-inch (Big Daddy) piece of tape, as shown in Figure 3.9, in the alternative technique below.

metadata.header_path: /Knee Malalignment Taping Technique/
/Knee Malalignment Taping Technique/

__data__.text:
### Taping Procedure: Knee Malalignment (Standard)

| Step | Application Path | Position | Stretch % |
|------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Patient is long sitting with knee straight | Long sitting, knee straight | — |
| 2 | Apply a smaller “I” strip from the lateral side of the knee (LCL), apply a medial glide to the lateral side of the patella, increase tension to 50–75%, and apply over the patella to maintain the gliding motion | Long sitting, knee straight | 50–75% |
| 3 | Heat activate the glue | — | — |

> **Figure 3.8**: a: Apply the tape (with tension) from the lateral side and finish medially, whilst applying pressure to the patella from the thumb. b: Finished position.

> **Figure 3.9**: The 4-inch tape (Big Daddy) has been specifically designed for this taping application.

Patellofemoral syndrome (knee pain)

metadata.header_path: /Knee Malalignment Taping Technique/
/Knee Malalignment Taping Technique/

__data__.text:
### Taping Procedure: Knee Malalignment (Alternative Technique)

| Step | Application Path | Position | Stretch % |
|------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Apply the tape from the lateral aspect of the patella, near the lateral collateral ligament (LCL) | Long sitting, knee straight | — |
| 2 | With the thumb, apply a medial glide to the lateral side of the patella, increase tension to the superior part of the tape to 50–75% and apply over the patella | Long sitting, knee straight | 50–75% (superior part) |
| 3 | With the thumb, apply a medial glide to the lateral side of the patella, increase tension to the inferior part of the tape to 50–75% and apply over the patella | Long sitting, knee straight | 50–75% (inferior part) |
| 4 | Heat activate the glue | — | — |

> **Figure 3.10**: First application of the tape, from the lateral side of the knee.

> **Figure 3.11**: Tension the superior part of the tape with medial glide from the thumb.

> **Figure 3.12**: Apply tension to the inferior part of the tape with medial glide from the thumb.

Once you have applied the tape then the knee should look like Figure 3.13.

> **Figure 3.13**: How it should look after the tape has been applied.

---

metadata.header_path: /Knee Malalignment Taping Technique/
/Lateral Knee Pain: Iliotibial Band Friction Syndrome / Lateral Meniscus / Lateral Collateral Ligament (LCL)/

__data__.text:
## Lateral Knee Pain: Iliotibial Band Friction Syndrome / Lateral Meniscus / Lateral Collateral Ligament (LCL)

I personally believe that everyone who enjoys running, whether competitively or recreationally, will, at some stage in their life, experience one of these conditions to the lateral side of their knee to varying degrees. There are many reasons why an athlete or patient presents with lateral knee pain and the most common reason is iliotibial band friction syndrome (ITBFS). The reason why the patient is experiencing lateral knee pain could simply be from wearing incorrect footwear, especially among those who are new to running.

It is not within the scope of this book to discuss all causes of lateral knee pain (Fig. 3.14); however, the following kinesiology taping technique will help reduce the patient’s presenting pain. Through this fantastic technique, it is possible to provide enough pain relief to enable the patient/athlete to continue participating in the sport they love, as well as taking part in a physical therapy assessment (to identify the underlying cause(s)) and subsequent treatment.

> **Figure 3.14**: ITBFS/lateral meniscus and the LCL. Iliotibial band friction syndrome. Gerdy’s tubercle. Lateral collateral ligament (LCL). Lateral meniscus.

metadata.header_path: /Lateral Knee Pain: Iliotibial Band Friction Syndrome / Lateral Meniscus / Lateral Collateral Ligament (LCL)/

__data__.text:
### Taping Procedure: Lateral Knee Pain

| Step | Application Path | Position | Stretch % |
|------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Apply an “I” strip from the Gerdy’s tubercle (insertion of IT band), cross the lateral femoral condyle, and finish along the length of the IT band | Side-lying, knee flexed, hip flexed and adducted | Little to no stretch |
| 2 | Using one small “I” strip, apply across the area of pain to the lateral femoral condyle | Same as above | 75–100% |
| 3 | Using another small “I” strip, place across the area of the other “I” strip to form an “X” shape across the IT band on the lateral femoral condyle | Same as above | 75–100% |
| 4 | Heat activate the glue | — | — |

> **Figure 3.15**: First application starting from Gerdy’s tubercle and finishing along the IT band.

> **Figure 3.16**: Second application of the tape using one small “I” strip across the area of pain.

> **Figure 3.17**: Third application of the tape using a small “I” strip to form an “X” shape across the IT band on the lateral femoral condyle.

Runner’s knee/iliotibial band friction syndrome

---

metadata.header_path: /Lateral Knee Pain: Iliotibial Band Friction Syndrome / Lateral Meniscus / Lateral Collateral Ligament (LCL)/
/Medial Knee Pain: Medial Collateral Ligament / Medial Meniscus/

__data__.text:
## Medial Knee Pain: Medial Collateral Ligament / Medial Meniscus

Medial collateral ligament (MCL) sprains and tears, as well as injuries to the medial meniscus, are considered to be some of the most common injuries, especially in sports and activities like football and skiing. The MCL, in particular, restricts the motion of a movement called “valgus.” This means the knee is restricted from moving inwards, so any excessive force in this plane will often result in the ligament becoming stretched and subsequently torn (or sprained) as well as a potential tear to the medial meniscus (Fig. 3.18).

> **Figure 3.18**: MCL sprain/medial meniscus tear.

metadata.header_path: /Medial Knee Pain: Medial Collateral Ligament / Medial Meniscus/

__data__.text:
### Taping Procedure: Medial Knee Pain

| Step | Application Path | Position | Stretch % |
|------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Apply an “I” strip across the medial aspect of the knee, starting from the distal inferior attachment onto the tibia and finishing on the proximal attachment to the femur | Knee at 90° flexion | Little to no stretch |
| 2 | Using one small “I” strip, apply across the area of pain | Same as above | 75–100% |
| 3 | Using another small “I” strip, place across the area of the other “I” strip to form an “X” shape across the MCL | Same as above | 75–100% |
| 4 | Heat activate the glue | — | — |

> **Figure 3.19**: First application to the MCL.

> **Figure 3.20**: Second application of the tape using one small “I” strip across the area of pain.

> **Figure 3.21**: Third application of the tape using a small “I” strip to form an “X” shape across the MCL.

Medial knee pain (MCL sprain/medial meniscus)

metadata.header_path: /KINESIOLOGY TAPING TECHNIQUES FOR THE KNEE JOINT/

__data__.text: ## KINESIOLOGY TAPING TECHNIQUES FOR THE KNEE JOINT

metadata.header_path: /KINESIOLOGY TAPING TECHNIQUES FOR THE KNEE JOINT/

__data__.text:
### GENERAL KNEE PAIN / PATELLOFEMORAL PAIN SYNDROME

Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS) is a painful condition that can relate to a type of mal-tracking of the kneecap (patella) (see Fig. 3.1). There are many causes for this condition, such as an over-pronation of the subtalar joint (STJ) of the ankle and poor foot biomechanics. Weakness of the inner quadriceps muscle (called the vastus medialis) can also contribute to PFPS, especially the vastus medialis oblique (VMO) fibers, which are thought to atrophy due to pain and minimal swelling. In addition, weak gluteus medius (Gmed) and gluteus maximus (Gmax) can cause this type of knee pain. The knee joint is therefore what I refer to as “a weak link in the kinetic chain,” and typically the presentation of the pain is not where the problem lies.

> **Figure 3.1**: General knee pain/PFPS.

1. The patient is asked to adopt a long sitting position with their knee at 90° of flexion. Next attach a “Y” strip from the superior aspect of the patella and apply the tape, with no stretch, to the medial and lateral sides of the patella. Finish by crossing over the tibial tuberosity (Fig. 3.2).

> **Figure 3.2**: First application of the “Y” strip starting from the superior aspect of the patella and finishing at the tibial tuberosity.

2. Apply a “Y” strip from the tibial tuberosity and lay the tape medially and laterally around the patella so that it overlaps the first “Y” application. Apply with little to no stretch and finish near the quadriceps tendon (Fig. 3.3).

> **Figure 3.3**: Second application of the “Y” strip starting from the tibial tuberosity and finishing at the starting point of the first application.

3. Heat activate the glue.

4. Once the glue has been heat activated, lower the limb back down to the couch and observe the “wrinkling” of the tape. This illustrates the effect the kinesiology tape is having on the underlying soft tissues through its unique lifting motion.

FULL KNEE TAPING: PFPS/
PATELLAR TENDINOPATHY/
OSGOOD-SCHLATTER’S  
DISEASE/BURSITIS
Any athlete who presents with pain inferior 
to the patella may also have pain in other soft 
tissues, such as the soft-tissue structure known 
as the patellar tendon. However, the pain 
could be due to other associated structures 
that sit on top of the tibial plateau, e.g., fat pad, 
infrapatellar bursa, and even the meniscus.

If, for example, a basketball player 
presents with pain to this area then it can 
be assumed that they have a condition 
called “jumper’s knee,” which is simply 
a tendinopathy of the patellar tendon. 
If, however, a 14-year-old footballer comes 
to the clinic with pain on the insertion of 
the patellar tendon at the attachment to 
the tibial tuberosity, then this is more than 
likely Osgood-Schlatter’s disease (Fig. 3.4).
This following technique is known as “full 
knee taping” as it has the ability to give a 
bit more stability without restricting the 
movement. It is an exceptional technique 
that can be quickly incorporated into your 
treatment program to offload the pain 
localized at the inferior aspect of the patella, 
as well as controlling the position of the 
patellofemoral joint.
1.	 Ask the patient to adopt a long sitting 
position and place their knee into 90° 
of flexion. Apply a small “I” strip across 
the patellar tendon at 50–75% stretch 
(Fig. 3.5).
Figure 3.5  Small “I” strip applied across the 
patellar tendon.
2.	 Apply an “I” strip from the lateral aspect 
of the thigh, with a 25% stretch of the tape, 
toward the patella. Then apply 50% stretch 
from the lateral side of the patella to the 
anterior aspect of the knee, and finish 
by crossing the tibial tuberosity with no 
stretch at the end of the tape (Fig. 3.6).
3.	 This next technique is similar to the one 
above, but this time apply an “I” strip 
from the medial aspect of the thigh, 
with 25% stretch of the tape, toward the 
patella. Then apply 50% stretch from 
the medial side of the patella to the 
front of the knee, and crossing the tibial 
tuberosity, with no stretch at the end of 
the tape (Fig. 3.7).
Figure 3.4  General knee pain/PFPS.

KNEE MALALIGNMENT TAPING 
TECHNIQUE 
The two techniques demonstrated below are 
potentially very simple in one respect because 
only one piece of tape is required. I do find in 
practice that sometimes the simple approach 
can work very well. Just remember that the 
majority of knee pain is merely a symptom, 
and the causative factor for the patient’s pain 
might be in another structure, such as the hip 
joint, or possibly over-pronation of the foot.
The application of a single piece of tape 
can be done on its own with a 2-inch small 
“I” strip. The technique can also be applied 
in combination with the full knee taping, as 
described below. Another option is to use the 
4-inch (Big Daddy) piece of tape, as shown in 
Figure 3.9, in the alternative technique below.
1.	 Patient is long sitting with their knee 
straight, as this will unlock the patella 
from the trochlear groove and will 
allow the patella to be glided in a medial 
direction.
2.	 Apply a smaller “I” strip from the 
lateral side of the knee (LCL), and with 
the thumb apply a medial glide to the 
lateral side of the patella. At the same 
time increase the tension to the tape 
to around 50–75%, and apply over the 
patella to maintain the gliding motion, 
as shown by Figure 3.8(a&b).

Figure 3.9  The 4-inch tape (Big Daddy) has been 
specifically designed for this taping application.
3.	 Heat activate the glue.

3.	 Heat activate the glue.
Patellofemoral 
syndrome (knee pain)
Alternative technique
This is similar to the technique 
demonstrated above; however, this time a 
larger piece of tape (4 inches—Big Daddy)  
is used and is cut into the shape shown  
in Figure 3.9.
Figure 3.8  a: Apply the tape (with tension) from the lateral side and finish medially, whilst 
applying pressure to the patella from the thumb. b: Finished position.
a
b

1.	 The tape is first applied from the lateral 
aspect of the patella, near the lateral 
collateral ligament (LCL), as shown in 
Figure 3.10.
Figure 3.10  First application of the tape, from 
the lateral side of the knee.
2.	 With the thumb, apply a medial glide 
to the lateral side of the patella. At the 
same time increase the tension to the 
superior part of the tape to around 
50–75% and apply over the patella to 
maintain the gliding motion, as shown 
in Figure 3.11.
3.	 Use the same procedure as above, and 
with the thumb apply a medial glide 
to the lateral side of the patella and 
increase the tension to the inferior 
part of the tape to around 50–75% 
and apply over the patella, as shown 
in Figure 3.12.
Figure 3.11  Tension the superior part of the

of the tape with medial glide from the thumb.

Once you have applied the tape then the

to the lateral side of their knee to varying 
degrees. There are many reasons why an 
athlete or patient presents with lateral 
knee pain and the most common reason is 
iliotibial band friction syndrome (ITBFS). 
The reason why the patient is experiencing 
lateral knee pain could simply be from 
wearing incorrect footwear, especially 
among those who are new to running.
It is not within the scope of this book

taping technique will help reduce the patient’s 
presenting pain. Through this fantastic 
technique, it is possible to provide enough

continue participating in the sport they love, 
as well as taking part in a physical therapy 
assessment (to identify the underlying 
cause(s)) and subsequent treatment.

on their side and place their knee into

Medial collateral ligament (MCL) sprains 
and tears, as well as injuries to the medial 
meniscus, are considered to be some of the 
most common injuries, especially in sports 
and activities like football and skiing. The 
MCL, in particular, restricts the motion of 
a movement called “valgus.” This means the 
knee is restricted from moving inwards, so 
any excessive force in this plane will often 
result in the ligament becoming stretched 
and subsequently torn (or sprained) as well 
as a potential tear to the medial meniscus 
(Fig. 3.18).
Medial collateral

Figure 3.21  Third application of the tape 
using a small “I” strip to form an “X” shape 
across the MCL.
4.	 Heat activate the glue.
Medial knee pain 
(MCL sprain/medial 
meniscus)

C H A P T E R  3
KINESIOLOGY TAPING TECHNIQUES 
FOR THE KNEE JOINT
GENERAL KNEE PAIN/
PATELLOFEMORAL PAIN 
SYNDROME
Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS) is a 
painful condition that can relate to a type 
of mal-tracking of the kneecap (patella) 
(see Fig. 3.1). There are many causes for 
this condition, such as an over-pronation 
of the subtalar joint (STJ) of the ankle 
and poor foot biomechanics. Weakness 
of the inner quadriceps muscle (called 
the vastus medialis) can also contribute 
to PFPS, especially the vastus medialis 
oblique (VMO) fibers, which are thought to 
atrophy due to pain and minimal swelling.

In addition, weak gluteus medius (Gmed) 
and gluteus maximus (Gmax) can cause this 
type of knee pain. The knee joint is therefore 
what I refer to as “a weak link in the kinetic 
chain,” and typically the presentation of the 
pain is not where the problem lies.
Patellofemoral
pain syndrome
(PFPS)
Figure 3.1  General knee pain/PFPS.

1.	 The patient is asked to adopt a long 
sitting position with their knee at 90° 
of flexion. Next attach a “Y” strip from 
the superior aspect of the patella and 
apply the tape, with no stretch, to the 
medial and lateral sides of the patella. 
Finish by crossing over the tibial 
tuberosity (Fig. 3.2).
Figure 3.2  First application of the “Y” strip 
starting from the superior aspect of the 
patella and finishing at the tibial tuberosity.

S
F
2.	 Apply a “Y” strip from the tibial 
tuberosity and lay the tape medially and 
laterally around the patella so that it 
overlaps the first “Y” application. Apply 
with little to no stretch and finish near 
the quadriceps tendon (Fig. 3.3). 
S
F
Figure 3.3  Second application of the “Y” strip  
starting from the tibial tuberosity and  
finishing at the starting point of the 
first application.
3.	 Heat activate the glue.
4.	 Once the glue has been heat activated, 
lower the limb back down to the couch 
and observe the “wrinkling” of the tape. 
This illustrates the effect the kinesiology 
tape is having on the underlying soft 
tissues through its unique lifting motion.
Knee pain 
(patellofemoral  
pain syndrome)
Chen et al.

Knee pain 
(patellofemoral  
pain syndrome)
Chen et al. (2008) conducted a study of the 
“Biomechanics effects of Kinesio Taping for 
persons with patellofemoral pain syndrome 
during stair climbing.” They concluded that 
this type of kinesiology taping could reduce 
pain and improve the ratio of VMO/vastus 
lateralis (VL) for the mechanism of patellar 
stability during stair climbing.
In general, PFPS is possibly one of the most 
common conditions that I have treated 
with some form of strapping and taping. 
This includes conventional athletic taping 
methods using zinc oxide (Z/O), as with the 
McConnell technique to control patellar 
alignment, as well as kinesiology taping. 
I like and use all the varieties of taping 
techniques as each of them has a unique 
effect upon the knee.

However, my choice 
at the present time for helping to reduce 
athletes’ and patients’ persistent knee pains 
is the kinesiology taping method.

FULL KNEE TAPING: PFPS/
PATELLAR TENDINOPATHY/
OSGOOD-SCHLATTER’S  
DISEASE/BURSITIS
Any athlete who presents with pain inferior 
to the patella may also have pain in other soft 
tissues, such as the soft-tissue structure known 
as the patellar tendon. However, the pain 
could be due to other associated structures 
that sit on top of the tibial plateau, e.g., fat pad, 
infrapatellar bursa, and even the meniscus.

If, for example, a basketball player 
presents with pain to this area then it can 
be assumed that they have a condition 
called “jumper’s knee,” which is simply 
a tendinopathy of the patellar tendon. 
If, however, a 14-year-old footballer comes 
to the clinic with pain on the insertion of 
the patellar tendon at the attachment to 
the tibial tuberosity, then this is more than 
likely Osgood-Schlatter’s disease (Fig. 3.4).
This following technique is known as “full 
knee taping” as it has the ability to give a 
bit more stability without restricting the 
movement. It is an exceptional technique 
that can be quickly incorporated into your 
treatment program to offload the pain 
localized at the inferior aspect of the patella, 
as well as controlling the position of the 
patellofemoral joint.
1.	 Ask the patient to adopt a long sitting 
position and place their knee into 90° 
of flexion.

Apply a small “I” strip across 
the patellar tendon at 50–75% stretch 
(Fig. 3.5).
Figure 3.5  Small “I” strip applied across the 
patellar tendon.
2.	 Apply an “I” strip from the lateral aspect 
of the thigh, with a 25% stretch of the tape, 
toward the patella. Then apply 50% stretch 
from the lateral side of the patella to the 
anterior aspect of the knee, and finish 
by crossing the tibial tuberosity with no 
stretch at the end of the tape (Fig. 3.6).
3.	 This next technique is similar to the one 
above, but this time apply an “I” strip 
from the medial aspect of the thigh, 
with 25% stretch of the tape, toward the 
patella.

Then apply 50% stretch from 
the medial side of the patella to the 
front of the knee, and crossing the tibial 
tuberosity, with no stretch at the end of 
the tape (Fig. 3.7).
Figure 3.4  General knee pain/PFPS.
Patellar  
tendinopathy/ 
bursitis
Patellofemoral  
pain syndrome 
(PFPS)
Osgood-  
Schlatter’s 
disease

Figure 3.6  An “I” strip is applied from the 
lateral aspect of the thigh to the tibial 
tuberosity.
Figure 3.7  An “I” strip is applied from the 
medial aspect of the thigh to the tibial 
tuberosity.
4.	 Heat activate the glue.

4.	 Heat activate the glue.
Knee pain 
(patellofemoral 
syndrome/Osgood- 
Schlatter’s disease)
KNEE MALALIGNMENT TAPING 
TECHNIQUE 
The two techniques demonstrated below are 
potentially very simple in one respect because 
only one piece of tape is required. I do find in 
practice that sometimes the simple approach 
can work very well. Just remember that the 
majority of knee pain is merely a symptom, 
and the causative factor for the patient’s pain 
might be in another structure, such as the hip 
joint, or possibly over-pronation of the foot.
The application of a single piece of tape 
can be done on its own with a 2-inch small 
“I” strip. The technique can also be applied 
in combination with the full knee taping, as 
described below. Another option is to use the 
4-inch (Big Daddy) piece of tape, as shown in 
Figure 3.9, in the alternative technique below.

1.	 Patient is long sitting with their knee 
straight, as this will unlock the patella 
from the trochlear groove and will 
allow the patella to be glided in a medial 
direction.
2.	 Apply a smaller “I” strip from the 
lateral side of the knee (LCL), and with 
the thumb apply a medial glide to the 
lateral side of the patella. At the same 
time increase the tension to the tape 
to around 50–75%, and apply over the 
patella to maintain the gliding motion, 
as shown by Figure 3.8(a&b).

Figure 3.9  The 4-inch tape (Big Daddy) has been 
specifically designed for this taping application.
3.	 Heat activate the glue.

3.	 Heat activate the glue.
Patellofemoral 
syndrome (knee pain)

demonstrated above; however, this time a 
larger piece of tape (4 inches—Big Daddy)  
is used and is cut into the shape shown  
in Figure 3.9.
Figure 3.8  a: Apply the tape (with tension) from the lateral side and finish medially, whilst 
applying pressure to the patella from the thumb. b: Finished position.

aspect of the patella, near the lateral 
collateral ligament (LCL), as shown in 
Figure 3.10.
Figure 3.10  First application of the tape, from 
the lateral side of the knee.
2.	 With the thumb, apply a medial glide 
to the lateral side of the patella.

With the thumb, apply a medial glide 
to the lateral side of the patella. At the 
same time increase the tension to the 
superior part of the tape to around 
50–75% and apply over the patella to 
maintain the gliding motion, as shown 
in Figure 3.11.
3.	 Use the same procedure as above, and 
with the thumb apply a medial glide 
to the lateral side of the patella and 
increase the tension to the inferior 
part of the tape to around 50–75% 
and apply over the patella, as shown 
in Figure 3.12.
Figure 3.11  Tension the superior part of the

LATERAL KNEE PAIN: ILIOTIBIAL 
BAND FRICTION SYNDROME/
LATERAL MENISCUS/LATERAL 
COLLATERAL LIGAMENT (LCL)
I personally believe that everyone who 
enjoys running, whether competitively or 
recreationally, will, at some stage in their 
life, experience one of these conditions 
to the lateral side of their knee to varying 
degrees. There are many reasons why an 
athlete or patient presents with lateral 
knee pain and the most common reason is 
iliotibial band friction syndrome (ITBFS). 
The reason why the patient is experiencing 
lateral knee pain could simply be from 
wearing incorrect footwear, especially 
among those who are new to running.
It is not within the scope of this book 
to discuss all causes of lateral knee pain 
(Fig. 3.14); however, the following kinesiology 
taping technique will help reduce the patient’s 
presenting pain.

Through this fantastic 
technique, it is possible to provide enough 
pain relief to enable the patient/athlete to 
continue participating in the sport they love, 
as well as taking part in a physical therapy 
assessment (to identify the underlying 
cause(s)) and subsequent treatment.
Figure 3.14  ITBFS/lateral meniscus and the LCL.
Iliotibial band
friction syndrome
Gerdy’s tubercle
Lateral collateral 
ligament (LCL)
Lateral meniscus

1.	 Ask the patient to adopt a position 
on their side and place their knee into 
flexion, while their hip is flexed and 
adducted so that the iliotibial (IT) band 
is on stretch. Apply an “I” strip, with little 
to no stretch, from the Gerdy’s tubercle 
(insertion of IT band) and cross the 
lateral femoral condyle to finish along the 
length of the IT band (Fig. 3.15).

3.15).

from Gerdy’s tubercle and finishing along  
the IT band.
2.	 Using one small “I” strip, apply 75–100% 
stretch and place this piece of tape 
across the area of pain to the lateral 
femoral condyle (Fig. 3.16).
3.	 Using another small “I” strip, place this 
one with 75–100% stretch across the area 
of the other “I” strip, already positioned 
across the area of pain, so that it looks 
like an “X” once completed (Fig. 3.17).

MEDIAL KNEE PAIN: MEDIAL 
COLLATERAL LIGAMENT/ 
MEDIAL MENISCUS
Medial collateral ligament (MCL) sprains 
and tears, as well as injuries to the medial 
meniscus, are considered to be some of the 
most common injuries, especially in sports 
and activities like football and skiing.

The 
MCL, in particular, restricts the motion of 
a movement called “valgus.” This means the 
knee is restricted from moving inwards, so 
any excessive force in this plane will often 
result in the ligament becoming stretched 
and subsequently torn (or sprained) as well 
as a potential tear to the medial meniscus 
(Fig. 3.18).
Medial collateral
ligament sprain 
Medial meniscus
Figure 3.18  MCL sprain/medial meniscus tear.
1.	 The patient is asked to place their knee 
at 90° of flexion. Apply an “I” strip, with 
little to no stretch, across the medial 
aspect of the knee and starting from the 
distal inferior attachment onto the tibia. 
Finish on the proximal attachment to the 
femur, as shown by Figure 3.19.

stretch and place this piece of tape across

3.	 Using another small “I” strip, place

the area of the other “I” strip, already 
positioned across the area of pain, so 
that it looks like an “X” once completed 
(Fig. 3.21).
